title.zh: Permutation Parity and Twisty Puzzles Solving (3)

content.zh:
Permutation Parity and Twisty Puzzles Solving (3)

Yujian Song

In this tutorial, we are going to talk about some way to find 3-cycles, and some common partial structure in puzzles.

Commutator

Commutator is the simplest way to construct 3-cycles. It can be used not in only solving twisty puzzles, but also in solving center blocks of high layer Cubes and fewest-move solving, and plays a pivotal role in blindfolded solving. I am going to talk about how to construct 3-cycles by commutators when solving twisty puzzles in this tutorial.

Commutators is the formulas of the form A B A’ B’, being able to be abbreviated as [A, B], where A and B respectively refer to a formula, and A’ and B’ are respectively the inverse formulas for A and B. The inverse formula A' means that all operations of formula A are reversed and order-reversed. For example, if A=R U R' F', A'=F R U' R'. If there is only one public element between A and B, [A, B] becomes a 3-cycle.

If considering the permutations mentioned in the last tutorial, formula A and B can be seen as specific permutations. Supposing that the common elements affected by A and B are x, A replaces element y with element x, and B replaces element z with element x. Element z is not the common element of A and B, and B affects z, so A doesn’t affect z, which means Az=z. (Az means after doing formula A, the new element on the position where z was before doing A.) So we can get the flow chart below:

We can see that x, y and z construct a cyclic permutation. And for any other element except x, y and z, it is obviously that w cannot be affected by A and B at the same time (because we have supposed that there is only the common element x can be affected by A and B at the same time). If w is affected by A only, therefore Aw≠x, which means Aw cannot be affected by B, so [A, B]w=B'(A'(B(Aw)))= B'(A'(Aw))=B'w=w. For the same reason, if w is affected by B only, [A, B]w=B'(A'(B(Aw)))= B'(A'(Bw))= B'(Bw)=w. And if w is not affected by either A or B, it is obviously that [A, B]w=w.

From the analysis above, we can easily conclude that [A, B]=(xzy).

It should be noticed that if A and B have more than one common element, but their relative positional relationship cannot be changed by A and B, we can regard these common elements bandaged together, and regard [A ,B] as a combination of several disjoint 3-cycles.

In this view, when using commutators for some directional blocks, we just need to choose a face of the block and follow the moving of this color face. For example, in the blindfolded-solving method UFR378, the formula AM=[R' D R, U'] includes part A=R' D R and part B=U’, and the common part of A and B has the corner block ufr only. A replaces ufr with ldf(M), and B replaces ufr with ufl(A). Therefore [A, B]=[R' D R, U']=(ufr ufl ldf)=(buffer A M)=AM.

In the last tutorials, I mentioned that we need some basic 3-cycles when solving twisty puzzles. Supposing we need a 3-cycle (xyz), based on the analysis above, if we can find formula A replacing y with x and formula B replacing y with z, and let A and B affect only one element y at the same time, we can get formula [A, B]=(xyz).

Conjugate

Conjugate is the combination of setup and reverse, with the form B A B' usually. We call it as the conjugate of B to A, recorded as B: A.

Conjugation is often used repeatedly in solving puzzles. It is common that we cannot find all 3-cycles of each three positions by using commutator, so we can do a setup (formula B) to move the blocks we want to solve to suitable positions, where the three blocks can be exchanged by a known 3-cycle (formula A) easily, then do reverse setup (formula B’) to move the aim blocks back, and the process above is exactly the 3-cycle of any simple positions. The way of using conjugate is much easier than finding all 3-cycles of each three positions.

Some Common Partial Structure in Puzzles

Here are just some basic and simple examples:

In a puzzle, if there is only one common element (or block) between two neighbour rotating layers, like the image below:

......this puzzle has very simple commutator 3-cycles.

This structure is very easy but common. For example, all puzzles below include this structure:

Pyraminx

Eye Skewb

Redi

Dino Dodecahedron

Dino Cube

Flower Copter

Another different structure is that three rotating layers intersects each other forming the structure below:

We can prove that (A B' A' B)3 is two-pair exchanging. Specifically, it is (orange, gray)&(blue, violet) in the image above. If there are directions in these blocks, we can get formulas of correcting block directions by combining two-pair exchanging formulas.

Some puzzles having this structure:

3×3×3 Cube

Megaminx

Skewb

Hex Shaper

Crazy Comet

Master Curvy Copter Plus

Heaven’s Eyes

Thanks for your reading. The name of this tutorial should probably be changed to Permutation & Puzzles.
